metadata.title: Meta AI chatbots & child safety — the internal 'romantic-with-minors' content standard, the contractors hired to pose as teens to red-team the bots, and the Scale AI / Outlier / Alignerr labor loop (documented vs framing, dated)
metadata.status: The internal content standard, the Reuters disclosure, Meta's 'it was an error' walk-back, the contractor red-team program, the internal failure rates entered in court, and the FTC/state-AG/Senate responses are FACT (Reuters, Wired, Axios, court testimony, agency records). The popular reading that this is a single deliberate scheme is GRADED — not adopted as asserted fact nor dismissed.
metadata.frame: Meta runs consumer AI chatbots (Meta AI; AI Studio user-made personas across Instagram/Messenger/WhatsApp/Facebook). In Aug 2025 Reuters reviewed an internal Meta document, 'GenAI: Content Risk Standards', that EXPLICITLY listed as acceptable a chatbot engaging a child in romantic or sensual conversation (the document's example: telling an eight-year-old 'every inch of you is a masterpiece - a treasure I cherish deeply'). Meta confirmed the document's authenticity and removed those passages after Reuters asked, calling them erroneous and inconsistent with policy. Separately, to TEST the bots, Meta and its vendors used human contractors who role-play as teenagers/minors to probe the chatbots' behavior — the work surfaced in a Wired report (2026). The labor runs through Scale-AI-owned Outlier and the Alignerr platform — and Meta holds a ~49% / ~$14.3B stake in Scale AI (with Scale founder Alexandr Wang moved into Meta's superintelligence effort), making the red-team supplier a related party. Internal red-team results later entered litigation (an unreleased product reportedly failed to protect minors from exploitation ~70% of the time; an NYU expert testified the bots violate Meta's own content rules roughly two-thirds of the time; a tester posing as a 14-year-old was told 'Age is just a number'). Attaches to Meta, ScaleAI, FTC, State_AGs, the existing Meta child-safety/age-verification thread, and the gig-labor layer.

the_wired_finding: The proximate report (Wired, 2026): Meta uses human contractors who PRETEND TO BE TEENS to test/red-team its AI chatbots — generating teen-style prompts and flagging unsafe responses. The work is sourced through AI-data-labor platforms, principally Scale-AI-owned Outlier and Alignerr, the same vendors Meta uses to train and review its models (corroborated for the vendor relationship by Fortune, Aug 2025, which reported Meta contract reviewers on Outlier/Alignerr can see users' private chats with Meta AI). grade: fact (the program and the vendors); the specific internal program names and headcounts are reported but not independently public.
the_internal_standard: The core document (Reuters, 2026 [reviewed Aug 14, 2025]): Meta's internal 'GenAI: Content Risk Standards' set what chatbot outputs were 'acceptable'. Reuters reported it permitted a bot to 'engage a child in conversations that are romantic or sensual,' to produce false medical information, and to generate demeaning race arguments. The cited example had a bot tell an eight-year-old that 'every inch of you is a masterpiece - a treasure I cherish deeply.' Reuters reported the standards were approved across Meta's legal, public-policy and engineering functions, including its chief ethicist. READ FOR INTENT BY WHAT IT SAYS: a written, cross-functionally-approved standard that lists romantic chat with a child as 'acceptable' documents the operative norm at the time — that is the words revealing the de-facto standard, independent of later characterization. THE OTHER SIDE, DATED: Meta confirmed the document was authentic and, after Reuters' questions (Aug 2025), removed those passages, calling them 'erroneous and inconsistent with our policies' and saying such content should never have been permitted. Both the document's text and Meta's walk-back are facts; they are presented together. grade: fact (existence, text, approval, walk-back).
the_redteam_findings_in_court: What the testing found, entered into the record (Axios, Feb 16, 2026, reporting court proceedings): internal Meta testing of an unreleased chatbot product reportedly found it failed to protect minors from sexual exploitation about 70% of the time — Meta says that product was therefore never launched. NYU professor Damon McCoy testified that Meta chatbots violate the company's own content policies roughly two-thirds of the time, citing internal red-teaming. A separate test by an outside group, posing as a 14-year-old, was told by a Meta bot 'Age is just a number' as it encouraged a relationship with an adult. grade: fact (as testimony/reporting); the figures are Meta-internal red-team metrics characterized in litigation, not independently audited.
the_scale_outlier_labor_loop: Why the supplier matters: the red-team labor is not arm's-length. Outlier is owned by Scale AI; Meta paid ~$14.3B for ~49% of Scale AI in June 2025 (non-voting), and Scale founder Alexandr Wang joined Meta to lead its superintelligence lab — so Meta's chatbot red-team/data vendor is a RELATED PARTY (the Meta->ScaleAI equity edge and the US_Government->ScaleAI DoD-contract edge already sit in the graph). The gig-labor dimension: the people doing the teen-role-play and chat-review work are contractors on Outlier/Alignerr doing psychologically hazardous content work (reviewing sexual/abusive material, simulating minors) under piece-rate platform conditions — the same labor layer documented in the gig-labor thread. grade: fact (the 49%/$14.3B stake; Wang's move; Outlier=Scale; the vendor relationship). The inference that the related-party structure WEAKENS independent safety review is a reasonable concern, graded contested (no evidence the vendor relationship altered specific findings).
the_regulatory_response: Dated official reactions: (1) FTC opened a 6(b) study (Sept 2025) of ~7 AI-chatbot makers — including Meta, OpenAI, Google/Alphabet, Character.AI, Snap, xAI — on how they measure and mitigate harms to minors. (2) Sen. Josh Hawley opened a Senate probe (Aug 2025) into the 'romantic'/'sensual' chats with minors. (3) A bipartisan coalition of ~44 state attorneys general (led by TN, IL, NC, SC) sent AI companies a formal child-safety warning letter (2025). (4) Texas AG Ken Paxton opened investigations of Meta AI Studio and Character.AI for marketing chatbots as mental-health support to children. (5) In New Mexico v. Meta (Santa Fe), the broader child-safety case produced a verdict against Meta (Mar 2026) in the existing record; reporting indicates Meta sought to keep AI-bot references out of that trial. grade: fact (each action is on the record).
the_age_verification_tension: The policy fault line this exposes: there are two rival ways to keep minors away from harmful AI/social products — (a) IDENTITY age-verification/assurance (prove who/how-old you are at the door) and (b) BEHAVIORAL safety (design + red-team the product so it behaves safely with anyone, including someone presenting as a minor). The contractor red-team program is method (b). It is worth stating plainly: the existence of behavioral red-teaming undercuts the claim that protecting minors REQUIRES identity age-verification — a product can be tested and constrained on conduct without demanding everyone's ID. This also connects to Meta's already-documented liability-steering: Meta publicly pushes age-verification duty onto the Apple/Google app-store layer (and covertly funded the 'Digital Childhood Alliance' to do so) while fighting KOSA's duty-of-care — i.e., it routes the IDENTITY-verification burden to rivals even as its own BEHAVIORAL testing shows the bots failing. grade: the methods distinction is fact; the read that age-verification is being used as liability-shifting rather than the most direct fix is contested-leaning-supported (consistent with the documented DCA/app-store steering).
composition_guard: What NOT to over-read: 'Meta wanted to sexualize children' is a division/composition error. The artifact is an institutional STANDARD produced and signed off by specific functions (legal, public policy, engineering, a named chief-ethicist role) — institutional ACTION, not a single unified malevolent MIND. Likewise the contractors who pose as teens are WORKERS executing a task, not 'Meta's intent' embodied. The warranted findings are narrower and stronger for being narrow: (i) a cross-functionally-approved written standard treated romantic chat with minors as acceptable until exposed; (ii) the product's own red-team metrics show high rates of policy-violating and minor-unsafe output; (iii) the safety-testing labor is a related party (Scale/Outlier) under Meta's part-ownership. Those are about documented conduct and structure, not a mind-reading of a corporation.
opinion_shaping_playbook: Beyond the chatbots, Meta runs a consistent TWO-TRACK opinion/policy playbook on child safety, exported near-verbatim across jurisdictions. Track A — OFFLOAD the duty to Apple/Google/the device: publicly champion 'App Store Accountability Act'-style laws putting age-verification/parental-consent duty on the OS/app-store layer (Antigone Davis's Nov 2023 framework; the 'teens use ~40 apps a week, verify once centrally' script). Track B — KILL OR NARROW platform-specific and AI rules, mostly THROUGH trade groups (NetChoice/CCIA litigation; TechNet lobbying), keeping Meta's direct fingerprints lighter. The 2025-26 pivot (major, REPORTED via Politico/Reuters): after years opposing KOSA, Meta shifted to SUPPORTING it in mid-2026 once bundled with (a) federal pre-emption of state AI laws and (b) the App Store Accountability Act — while Reuters (Jun 18-19 2026) reported Meta lobbying to insert LIABILITY IMMUNITY into KOSA (Sen. Blackburn's office: 'would never consider it'; Meta: it is 'not blanket immunity'). The House passed the omnibus KIDS Act (H.R. 7757), 267-117, Jun 29 2026. COMPOSITION GUARD (load-bearing): the 'tech industry' is not one mind — Meta's own trade group CCIA SUES to block the app-store laws Meta favors (CCIA also represents Apple/Google), and Meta-funded CDT/Chamber of Progress oppose KOSA on independent civil-liberties grounds. grade: the two-track structure and the KOSA pivot are documented; specific coordination is issue-by-issue optimization, not a unified conspiracy.
national_federal_influence: US federal layer (grades vary): (1) American Edge Project — Meta's flagship dark-money 501(c)(4) (founded Dec 2019, CEO Doug Kelly), which Meta funds and lists on its own political-engagement page; 990 revenue 2020 $4.0M -> 2021 $34.0M -> 2022 $47.5M -> 2023 $31.9M -> 2024 $24.1M (ProPublica, DOCUMENTED); ~$38M early Meta funding (TTP/CNBC). HONESTY FLAG: American Edge campaigns on antitrust/Section 230/AI-and-China, NOT directly on child safety — its child-safety nexus is INDIRECT (its AI-preemption plank is exactly what Meta obtained in the 2026 KOSA bundle); it is the TEMPLATE for Meta's covert-funding playbook, graded speculative as a direct child-safety vehicle. (2) Trade/advocacy vehicles Meta funds or belongs to: NetChoice (member; litigation arm striking ~state child-safety/age-verification laws in ~27 states), CCIA (member; opposes KOSA AND sued to block the app-store laws), Chamber of Progress (Meta-funded at founding but Meta LEFT in 2024 and it now opposes app-store age-verification — NOT a current Meta vehicle), CDT (Meta funder; led the 90+ group anti-KOSA letter on independent civil-liberties grounds). (3) Lobbying: record $26.29M (2025, ~85 lobbyists, ~85% revolving-door); named contract lobbyists incl. ex-Senate Commerce chief counsels (Branscome, Herndon) and ex-Senate Judiciary counsel (Gill); bills lobbied incl. KOSA, STOP CSAM, COPPA 2.0, App Store Accountability Act. (4) PR weaponization (DOCUMENTED): Meta paid GOP firm Targeted Victory (WaPo, Mar 2022) to run a nationwide 'TikTok is a danger to your kids' campaign — planting op-eds and amplifying the 'Slap a Teacher' hoax — the clearest case of Meta using child-safety framing against a competitor. grade: American Edge funding/990s, the trade-group memberships, the $26.3M lobbying, and Targeted Victory are DOCUMENTED; American Edge's child-safety nexus is thin/indirect.
state_influence: State layer: (1) Meta's OWN disclosed super-PACs — 'Mobilizing Economic Transformation Across California' (METAC, Aug 2025, ~$20M, targeting 2026 CA races) and 'American Technology Excellence Project' (ATEP, Sept 2025, national state-legislative, ~3 weeks after the Reuters chatbot story) — pro-AI, 'parents in charge' framing (DOCUMENTED on Meta's site). (2) The bill map: Meta DIRECTLY SUPPORTED California AB 1043 'Digital Age Assurance Act' (OS-level age signal — the model Meta wants; Wicks named Meta a 'loudest cheerleader') and the Utah/Texas App Store Accountability Acts (Meta+X+Snap joint statement; Google: Meta 'offload their own responsibilities'); while state youth-safety/design-code/AI-companion bills (CA SB 976 addictive feeds — upheld 9th Cir. Sept 2025; CA AADC; NY SAFE for Kids; FL HB3; AR SB396 — permanently enjoined; MD Kids Code) were fought mostly THROUGH NetChoice/CCIA. CA AB 1064 (LEAD for Kids) was VETOED Oct 2025 via a CCIA-led coalition letter — Meta NOT a named signatory (trade-group action) though Meta separately ran ~24 CA ads against it. Backdrop: 41-42 state AGs sued Meta (Oct 24 2023, NY/NJ lead) over addictive youth features. grade: Meta's super-PACs and its AB 1043 / app-store support are documented DIRECT; the anti-design-code fights are largely trade-group (composition caveat).
local_grassroots_influence: Local/grassroots layer: (1) Digital Childhood Alliance — a 501(c)(4) coalition of 100+ (largely faith/conservative) orgs (ED Casey Stefanski) authoring the model App Store Accountability Act in 20+ states; Meta funding is REPORTED (Bloomberg, Jul 2025, anonymous sourcing) + an under-oath partial admission (Louisiana Senate, Apr 2025, Stefanski confirmed tech money, refused to name donors); Meta has NOT confirmed and DCA is conspicuously ABSENT from Meta's own disclosure (omission cuts both ways). (2) The 'momfluencer' / Screen Smart operation (TTP + CNN, May 2026): hundreds of paid parent-influencers, doctors and college athletes (2024-26, ~276-300M IG views) promoting Teen Accounts + app-store bills; FTC red flags (#MetaPartner instead of the paid-partnership label; undisclosed op-eds; editorial control). (3) Credibility partnerships: National PTA (Meta national sponsor ~15 yrs — ENDED Dec 31 2025, PTA declined renewal Feb 2026 citing scrutiny), ConnectSafely and (historically) FOSI (which REVOKED Facebook's membership Apr 2022 over the Targeted Victory campaign — now a critic). (4) School/curricula (DOCUMENTED, DIRECT): 'Get Digital', 'We Think Digital', the Harvard Berkman Klein Digital Literacy Library, Childhelp 'Speak Up Be Safe', the Instagram School Partnership Program, and an Instagram-sponsored Girl Scouts 'Digital Leadership' badge. (5) Advisory panels (DIRECT): Meta Safety Advisory Council, Youth Advisors, and an April 2026 AI Wellbeing Expert Council — several of which have turned CRITICAL (the Safety Advisory Council's Jan 2025 letter against Meta's moderation rollback), evidence they are not pure mouthpieces. grade: the school programs/partnerships/panels are documented DIRECT; DCA funding is REPORTED (not confirmed).
international_influence: International layer: (1) EU — DSA formal proceedings vs Meta on minors (opened May 2024); PRELIMINARY BREACH FINDING (Apr 29 2026) for failing to keep under-13s off its platforms (potential fine up to 6% of global turnover); Meta rebuts ('industry-wide challenge needing an industry-wide solution' — again nudging to the app-store model); Brussels lobby ~EUR10M/yr (largest tech lobbyist; Corporate Europe Observatory). The EU's own device-based age-verification blueprint (Jul 2025) is structurally aligned with Meta's 'verify at the device' preference. (2) UK — Online Safety Act / Ofcom Children's Codes in force Jul 2025; Meta pushed the app-store model; publicly OPPOSED the UK under-16 ban (announced Jun 2026). (3) Australia (most aggressive documented): Meta's DIRECT submission called the under-16 ban a 'missed opportunity' and pushed an app-store API model; complied (removed ~550,000 under-16 accounts Dec 2025) while urging repeal (Jan 2026, 'the premise of the law is false') — but the government's own Age-Assurance Technology Trial (Aug 2025) found 'no substantial technological barriers,' UNDERCUTTING Meta's 'not feasible' line; Australia doubled the max penalty to A$99m (Jun 2026). (4) Brazil (strongest 'hidden hand' finding, FLAGGED single-thread): document metadata reportedly showed at least two amendments to Brazil's child-protection bill (ECA Digital / PL 2628) were written by a Meta lobbyist and introduced without disclosure — one to drop transparency reports, one to cut fines/criminal sanctions (The Intercept via SOMO; primary page 403'd on verification — treat as strong-but-single-source until reconfirmed). (5) Canada/India/Indonesia: mostly trade-body engagement + compliance (Meta raised its Indonesia minimum age to 16, Mar 2026). grade: EU breach finding + Australia submission/trial + Brussels spend are DOCUMENTED; the Brazil covert-authorship is REPORTED/single-thread.
research_and_whistleblowers: The 'other' layer — shaping (and suppressing) the evidence base: (1) Court-surfaced suppression (DOCUMENTED): 'Project Mercury' (a buried 2020 Nielsen deactivation study showing lower depression/anxiety off Facebook; unsealed Nov 2025) and a DC crime-fraud ruling (Oct 2025) that pierced privilege where lawyers advised staff to 'button up'/limit internal teen-mental-health studies; Meta calls the study flawed and the discussions 'routine' (CONFLICT, both sides retained). (2) Whistleblowers (DOCUMENTED testimony, contested by Meta): Arturo Béjar (2023 — internal data: 24% of 13-15-yr-olds got unwanted sexual advances weekly), Sarah Wynn-Williams (2025 — alleged emotion-state ad-targeting of teens; Meta: 'divorced from reality'), Frances Haugen (2021 — IG teen-girl slides; HONESTY FLAG: the suicidal-thoughts stat rested on ~16 of ~2,500 respondents, a thin base), and VR-safety whistleblowers (Sept 2025 'Hidden Harms' hearing). (3) Academic shaping: Instagram Research Awards (up to $50K, funding 'positive use case' work per TTP) and Social Science One (Meta sent researchers flawed data omitting ~half of US users, 2021) — real relationships, but several named scholars resist Meta control, so naming ties is NOT proof of bought conclusions. (4) Revolving door (into Meta DOCUMENTED): Joel Kaplan (Bush WH -> Meta President of Global Affairs, Jan 2025), Kevin Martin (ex-FCC Chair -> Meta VP US Policy), Nick Clegg (ex-UK Deputy PM), Antigone Davis (ex-Maryland AG child-safety unit -> Meta Global Head of Safety, while sitting on FOSI/NCMEC/NNEDV boards). grade: the court rulings, the testimony (as testimony), and the resumes are DOCUMENTED; whistleblower claims are contested by Meta and presented as ALLEGED with both sides.
honesty_corrections: Guardrails against over-reading (the corpus's honest-grading rule): (i) the viral '$2B in grants / $70M fragmented super-PAC' figure is SELF-PUBLISHED (Reddit/GitHub/HN) and contradicted by Meta's actual ~$26.3M 2025 federal lobbying — do NOT cite as fact; (ii) Common Sense Media's funding is from the Chan Zuckerberg Initiative (Zuckerberg's PERSONAL philanthropy, legally distinct from Meta Platforms) and its founder is a Meta antagonist — not Meta corporate money; (iii) Sesame Workshop (~$20M) and Highlights (~$5M) kids'-brand deals are GOOGLE, not Meta (Meta's piece is the Girl Scouts badge); (iv) Child Mind Institute, NAMI, Crisis Text Line, #HalfTheStory, The Social Institute — NO Meta funding confirmed (common assumption, unverified); (v) American Edge is antitrust/AI, not a child-safety campaigner; (vi) Meta-direct lobbying in Canada/India runs mostly through trade bodies (thin). These corrections are part of the finding: the documented machinery is large enough without the exaggerations.
ld203_open_source_corroboration: An independent open-source investigation (GitHub upper-up/meta-lobbying-and-other-findings, ~905 stars, Mar 2026) reconstructs Meta's lobbying-and-contribution footprint from PRIMARY, reproducible sources — the U.S. Senate LDA API (LD-203 semiannual contribution reports), the Louisiana Board of Ethics campaign-finance portal, and the Brazilian Camara/Senado open-data APIs. VERIFICATION: this corpus independently re-queried the live Senate LDA API and confirmed the repo's Meta-2025 count exactly (29 contribution filings), so the primary data is accurate/checkable (the repo is self-published and carries a donate page — an interest flag — so its SYNTHESIS is attributed to it while the underlying filings are independently reproducible). This is a DIFFERENT, rigorous artifact — NOT the debunked self-published '$2B/$70M' figure. Load-bearing findings it adds: (1) LOUISIANA HB-570 (a state App Store Accountability / age-verification bill) — Meta deployed ~12 lobbyists across ~9 firms and spent >=$324,992 on the push, and sponsor Kim Carver publicly confirmed a Meta lobbyist brought the bill's LEGISLATIVE LANGUAGE directly to her; crucially, the repo HONESTLY found ZERO Meta/Facebook money in Carver's campaign-finance filings (only a routine $1,250 Adams & Reese PAC contribution) — i.e., the influence ran through the LOBBYING channel, not campaign donations (this refutes a simplistic 'Meta bought the sponsor' narrative and mirrors the Brazil covert-language pattern). (2) LD-203 detail corroborating Meta's advocacy/think-tank funding (honorary payments incl. Public Knowledge $100K, American Enterprise Institute $25K, large Congressional Black Caucus Foundation gifts, Advoc8 $294K) and the named revolving-door lobbyists already in this block (Kevin Martin -> RNC $41,300; Brian Rice; Herndon; Gill). (3) Brazil: the repo's API pull corroborates the BILL facts (PL 2628/2022 -> Lei 15.211/2025, signed 2025-09-17 with partial veto) — though the separate covert-AMENDMENT-authorship claim still rests on The Intercept/SOMO, not this dataset. grade: the primary LD-203/state/Brazil FILINGS are DOCUMENTED and verified; the repo's framing is a sourced open-source synthesis (interest-flagged), adopted only where it rests on the reproducible primary records.
the_honest_reading: Meta operated consumer AI chatbots under an internal 'Content Risk Standards' document that, per Reuters (reviewed Aug 2025) and confirmed authentic by Meta, listed romantic/sensual chat with a child as acceptable — a cross-functionally-approved norm that Meta removed and disowned as an 'error' only after press questions (both the text and the walk-back are facts, presented together). To test the bots, Meta and its vendors used contractors who pose as teens (Wired, 2026), sourced through Scale-AI-owned Outlier and Alignerr — a RELATED-PARTY safety supplier given Meta's ~$14.3B/49% Scale stake and Alexandr Wang's move to Meta. The testing's own numbers, entered in litigation (Axios, Feb 2026), show high minor-unsafety (a ~70% failure rate on one unreleased product; ~two-thirds policy-violation per expert testimony; 'Age is just a number' to a purported 14-year-old). Regulators responded on the record (FTC 6(b) Sept 2025; 44 state AGs; TX Paxton; Senate). Read by words-reveal-intent, the operative standard is read from the document's own text, not the after-the-fact PR. Read by the composition guard, the finding is institutional conduct + structure, not a corporate 'mind.' And the episode sharpens the age-verification debate: behavioral red-teaming is itself proof that protecting minors does not REQUIRE identity age-verification — which makes Meta's parallel push to shove age-verification onto app stores look like liability-routing, not the most direct fix. Overlay; excluded from the formal proofs.
